narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school only to have the door close can feel like a punch to the gut, and I'm truly sorry you're facing this right now. Our prediction model looked at your profile and estimated a 46% chance of not passing the bar exam, which is why we had to say no to your application. What stood out most was your Year 3 decile of 4, which was the biggest signal pulling toward that risk—it suggested your performance in the later, tougher years of school didn't quite keep pace with top performers. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 2, painting a picture of early struggles that the model saw as a foundational challenge carrying forward. Your LSAT score of 31 added to those concerns, as it fell short of the benchmarks that typically predict bar success in competitive programs. Coming from a middle-income family was another piece that nudged the odds downward, reflecting patterns we've seen where extra resources can make a difference in the grind of law school prep. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.3, while solid, didn't fully offset those trends, and factors like being white, female, and planning full-time study all layered in subtle ways toward higher risk in the model's view. It's not that any one thing doomed your chances—it's this combination that together sketched a story of hurdles the model couldn't ignore. You've got real strengths here, like that UGPA showing discipline, and this isn't the end of your path. Many turn predictions like this into fuel, retaking the LSAT or bolstering late grades to shift the narrative next time. Hang in there—you're capable of rewriting this story.
